title: Rimshot Grew Up: Rudiments, Loops, and Real Songs
excerpt: A few days after the build post, Rimshot has a rudiments library, practice loops, and full-song MIDI playback. My timing is tighter, my songs are more or less songs, and I have opinions about hi-hat indicators.
body:
A few days ago I posted about [Rimshot](https://github.com/pstricker/rimshot), the drum trainer I built because I wanted one and didn't love any of the ones that already existed. That post was mostly about the build: .NET 8, MIDI input, audio playback, the usual side-project victory lap.

This one is about what's happened since. The app has grown up, and so has my drumming. Slightly, in roughly that order.

## What's Actually Better About My Drumming

Two specific things have come along that I want to call out.

The first is timing. Not *I'm a metronome* timing, just the kind of consistency where my hits land where I'm aiming most of the time, and when they don't I can usually tell why. The monitor tab in Rimshot has been brutally helpful here. Watching your average offset drift in one direction across a phrase is a lot harder to argue with than the vague feeling that something was a bit off.

The second is that I can now play through full songs. Not perfectly, not at full speed for everything, but start-to-finish, holding the groove, not falling apart at fills. When I picked up sticks, that was not happening.

The rudiment I keep going back to is single and double stroke rolls. Boring, ungrateful work, the thing that actually moved everything else.

## The PAS Rudiments Library

The biggest addition to the app is a built-in library of all 40 PAS rudiments, organized by category, Rolls, Diddles, Flams, and Drags. Each cue carries its own sticking, L or R, with the left-hand strokes tinted slightly darker so you can read the pattern at a glance.

Rudiments are the scales of drumming. You don't play them on stage, you play them so you can play everything else. Single and double stroke rolls are the ones I keep coming back to. They look trivial on paper and aren't, especially as you push the BPM up and try to keep the doubles even.

On the tech side, each rudiment ships as structured data, so the sticking is a property of the cue, not a label painted on top of it. That makes the L/R notation reliable across tempo changes and lets the renderer color the strokes consistently.

## Practice Loops

This is the feature I've used the most. You drag a region anywhere on the timeline strip and the app loops it. The selection snaps to the 1/16 grid, so your loop boundaries always land on a sensible beat instead of half a hi-hat into nothing.

The drumming version of this is humbling. I'd watch a song play, identify the four bars I keep blowing, drag a loop around them, drop the BPM to 60, and discover that I couldn't play the part *slow* either. That's the whole point. Loops force you to confront the specific spot where you're guessing instead of playing.

The loop tells no lies. (Neither does the monitor tab, but I covered that one last time.)

The grid snap is a tiny piece of math. The behavior change in my practice is enormous.

## MIDI Import and Backing Tracks

You can drag and drop a MIDI file onto the app. The drum channel becomes scrolling cues. The non-drum channels play back through a SoundFont synth, so you're not drumming alone with a metronome, you're playing along with a band that doesn't get tired of you.

This is the feature that turned Rimshot from *metronome with extra steps* into something I actually play songs on. Practicing a groove in isolation is one thing; practicing it while bass and keys are happening underneath is a different sport. Time stops being abstract. Either you're with the band or you're not.

The synthesis is handled by [MeltySynth](https://github.com/sinshu/meltysynth), which preserves velocities, program changes, volume, pan, sustain, and pitch bend. In plain English: it sounds like the song, not a chiptune cover of the song.

The lane strip also auto-shrinks to only the drums the current piece actually uses. Fewer empty lanes, less visual noise, more focus on what matters.

## The Smaller Wins

A few features that are individually small and collectively a big deal.

**Auto Play mode** plays the cues for you, perfect timing, perfect dynamics. I underestimated this until I realized I'd been practicing patterns I hadn't actually heard correctly. Hearing the thing first, then trying it, is just better.

**Velocity-to-gain** maps how hard you hit to how loud the sample plays. Sounds obvious. Wasn't there at first. The day I turned it on, I realized I'd been playing every hit at the same dynamic because nothing was rewarding me for doing otherwise. Now I'm actually thinking about ghost notes and accents.

**Open hi-hat tracking** flips a closed (●) to an open (○) indicator the moment the pedal lifts. Tiny detail. Big difference for grooves that breathe, anything that opens the hat on the and-of-four feels right now in a way it didn't before.

## Honest Takes

**Loops changed my practice more than any other feature.** Every other addition is in service of either drumming faster or drumming more, and loops are the only one that consistently makes me drum *better*.

**Backing tracks were the difference between drilling and playing.** Drilling makes you precise on a click. Playing makes you musical. They're different skills, and I needed both.

**Rudiments are unsexy and absolutely the thing that moved my timing.** Every time I think I've outgrown them I find I haven't.

**The app keeps making me confront things I can't do yet.** That's the point of the whole exercise. If Rimshot ever stops doing this it means I've stopped growing or started lying to myself, neither of which is great.

## Try It

Source is at [github.com/pstricker/rimshot](https://github.com/pstricker/rimshot). There are pre-built installers for Windows and Linux now, no .NET runtime install required, just download and run. macOS is source-build for the moment.

If you're learning drums and want a tool that mostly stays out of your way, give it a shot. If you have feature requests or strong opinions about how rudiment cues should display, the issues tab is open.
